# Job Fransen (Growth, Motor Development and Aging)

<!-- vimeo: 1111477507 | track: English (auto-generated) -->

Keep your friends close, but your enemies closer. This classic line often hints to the strategic value of understanding an opponent. Well, I use this principle to create an assessment task that leveraged the power of AI to foster a critical thinking in my students at a time when critical thinking is easily lost to convenient thinking. The assessment was for a course on growth, motor development and aging in the discipline of sport and exercise science. The specific topic was a pervasive issue in youth sports called the maturity bias. This bias occurs when young athletes of varying biological maturity levels are grouped by chronological age, which often gives an advantage to those who are more biologically mature. This can lead to a host of problems including a higher dropout rate among less mature athletes who may become discouraged and a skewed selection process that prioritizes early developers over late bloomers with long-term potential. My students had to investigate solutions to this problem as part of an assessment that was worth about 40% of the total grades for this subject. The solutions we were interested in were things like grouping athletes by maturity status, also known as bio banding, or using maturity related correction factors. The issue is, however, that many of these solutions are not well covered in the literature, and hence, students have to develop critical thinking skills to evaluate that literature in light of practical and logical or logistical shortcomings. For these solutions. To tackle this challenge, I designed an assessment where students had to work with an AI collaborator but not as a helpful assistant. Instead, the AI was an adversarial collaborator. This approach, sometimes also called red teaming, is common in scientific discourse and in real life situations where critically examining one's own ideas is crucial for developing robust solutions. It's the process of deliberately challenging your own assumptions and arguments to find their weaknesses, ultimately strengthening one's final position. Here's how the assessment worked. In practice. First, students began by researching and developing their own arguments in favor of a particular solution for the maturity bias in youth sports. By using the scientific literature, for example, students might argue that bio bending is the most effective solution because it creates a more level playing field and reduces the risk of injury. Then they crafted specific prompts about which they were educated in a prior tutorial to get an AI such As check GPT to act as an adversarial partner. The AI's role was to provide counter arguments and critically reflect on the student's initial ideas using the bio bending example. The AI might argue that implementing bio banding is impractical for many sports due to the high cost and logistical complexities, or that it could lead to social and psychological issues for young athletes who are grouped differently from their friends. With both their initial arguments and the AI generated counter arguments in hand, students then had to synthesize this information. They were tasked with developing a practical application of a proposed solution for a sport of their choice, taking into account the strengths and the weaknesses of their initial ideas and the AI's counter-arguments. This process pushed them to move beyond a simple pro or con stance and develop a nuanced and well-rounded proposal, which is very representative of any proposal in sport. Finally, the students recorded a seven minute video. For the first five minutes, they presented their and their AI adversarial collaborators arguments for and against the solution, and then propose their final practical implementation and how it aligned with these arguments. The final two minutes were dedicated to reflecting on how their interaction with the AI adversary shaped their critical thinking and final proposal. This was a crucial component as it forced them to articulate how not only working alongside ai, but working alongside an adversarial AI partner had deepened their understanding of this complex topic. This approach to assessment design is unique in this context, despite the fact that adversarial collaboration has a longstanding presence in science in general. With this assessment, my goal was to move beyond simply using AI as a glorified search tool and instead demonstrate how it can be used as a powerful albeit adversarial collaborator, the challenge one's perhaps biased arguments, and therefore deepen one's understanding of complex topics. This method not only improved the quality of the student's final projects, but also resonated well with the students themselves. They argued it, prepared them well for the real world complexities of finding solutions to complex problems where every idea must be rigorously tested and debated for its scientific relevance, as well as its practical feasibility.
